tested_predictions.hypothesis: XGBoost will outperform Logistic Regression by ≥6 percentage points in macro_f1 because Total_EMI_per_month (MI=0.5471 vs F=1.04) provides strong nonlinear signal and Type_of_Loan missingness (11.41% missing, max_target_dist_diff=0.1951) is exploitable by tree splits which LR cannot capture
tested_predictions.basis: Top mutual information concentrated in EMI with negligible ANOVA F, plus MNAR pattern in Type_of_Loan

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Total_EMI_per_month will rank #1 in global SHAP importance with mean |SHAP| ≥1.8× that of Monthly_Inhand_Salary (#2) because its MI=0.5471 slightly exceeds salary MI=0.5187 while its low linear separability (F=1.04 vs F=1903.07) means tree models lean heavily on its nonlinear splits
tested_predictions.basis: Mutual_information(EMI)=0.5471 vs Mutual_information(salary)=0.5187 and ANOVA F(EMI)=1.04 vs F(salary)=1903.07

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Missingness indicator for Type_of_Loan will appear in the top-5 features by SHAP importance because its missing_pct=11.41% and max_target_dist_diff=0.1951 create a strong target-propagating split
tested_predictions.basis: MNAR suspect: Type_of_Loan missing correlates with target by 19.51pp

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Standard class will have the lowest recall (≤0.65) because its Delay_from_due_date mean=19.75 is sandwiched between Good (10.89) and Poor (29.74) on the strongest separator (F=11471.89), creating dual confusion boundaries
supported_conjectures.basis: Class means on Delay_from_due_date and F-statistic=11471.89 for that feature
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Per-class recall and confusion matrix at evaluate-models

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Within Standard misclassifications, Standard→Good will exceed Standard→Poor by ≥20% because Standard’s mean delay is Δ=8.86 days from Good but Δ=9.99 days from Poor, and salary mean is Δ=1147 lower than Good vs Δ=867 higher than Poor
supported_conjectures.basis: Distances in class means: Delay and Monthly_Inhand_Salary
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Confusion matrix breakdown by misclassification direction

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: The ratio Total_EMI_per_month/Monthly_Inhand_Salary will achieve MI ≥0.60 and rank in the top-5 SHAP features because it fuses the two highest-MI features which have low pairwise correlation (no |r|>0.8)
exploratory_leads.basis: Mutual_information(EMI)=0.5471, Mutual_information(salary)=0.5187, absence of high correlations
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Engineer the ratio feature, recompute MI and global SHAP ranks

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Log-transforming Num_Credit_Inquiries and Total_EMI_per_month will boost their linear separability, driving the ANOVA F-statistic for Num_Credit_Inquiries from 6.26 to ≥50 and enabling LR to leverage it
exploratory_leads.basis: High skewness: Num_Credit_Inquiries=9.7862, Total_EMI_per_month=7.1025; current F=6.26 for inquiries
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Apply log(x+1) transforms, rerun ANOVA and model evaluation

eda_hypotheses.model_selection_prediction: XGBoost will achieve macro_f1 ≥0.82, outperforming Logistic Regression by ≥6pp, because the top MI features Total_EMI_per_month and Monthly_Inhand_Salary (0.5471/0.5187) and the MNAR pattern in Type_of_Loan are captured by tree splits
eda_hypotheses.class_struggle_prediction: Standard will have the lowest recall (≤0.65) with Standard→Good as the dominant confusion pair due to its Delay_from_due_date mean=19.75 lying between Good and Poor

Good.diagnosis: Recall~0.79, highest among classes. Confusion matrix (XGBoost) shows 17.5% of Good samples misclassified as Standard vs 3.3% as Poor, indicating a thin boundary with Standard that features fail to separate.

Poor.diagnosis: Recall~0.77, moderate difficulty. In XGBoost, 13.6% of Poor samples go to Standard vs 9.1% to Good (1.5:1), suggesting some overlap with Standard but less severe than Standard’s errors.

Standard.diagnosis: Recall~0.61, lowest. Errors split almost evenly: 20.7% → Poor vs 18.7% → Good, reflecting its intermediate feature means in Delay and Salary and dual boundary ambiguity.

capacity_analysis.logistic_regression: Underfitting. Best C=0.016 indicates heavy regularisation; macro_f1=0.630 vs cv=0.619 (gap 1.1pp) and 5.2pp below XGBoost confirms inability to capture nonlinear interactions.
capacity_analysis.random_forest: Well-fitted. max_depth=12 and min_samples_split=3 capture necessary interactions; cv=0.672 vs test=0.682 (gap ~1pp, test>cv) indicates good generalization.
capacity_analysis.xgboost: Slight overfitting. max_depth=6, learning_rate=0.252; cv=0.665 vs test=0.683 (gap ~1.75pp). Learning curves show validation mlogloss plateau by ~30 rounds then drift up, signaling shallow signal and mild overtraining.

confidence_analysis.summary: The mean confidence gap between correct vs incorrect predictions grows from LR (~0.02) to RF (~0.064) to XGB (~0.078), showing XGBoost is most aware of its errors. LR’s narrow gap suggests weak calibration. Across models, Standard has the lowest confidence on both correct and wrong predictions, matching its high error rate.

training_diagnostics.learning_curve_interpretation: XGBoost training mlogloss steadily declines to ~0.41, while validation mlogloss plateaus at ~0.74 by round 30 and gradually increases thereafter, indicating slight overfitting and that the signal is learnable with shallow trees.

tested.original: XGBoost will outperform Logistic Regression by ≥6 percentage points in macro_f1 because Total_EMI_per_month provides strong nonlinear signal…
Total_EMI_per_month will rank #1 in global SHAP importance with mean |SHAP| ≥1.8× that of Monthly_Inhand_Salary…
Missingness indicator for Type_of_Loan will appear in the top-5 features by SHAP importance…
tested.actual: Feature not implemented and no SHAP data

supported.original: Standard class will have the lowest recall (≤0.65)…

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Introducing a feature capturing payment delay variance will reduce Good→Standard confusion by ≥20%.
tested_predictions.basis: Good→Standard misclassification ratio ~5.3:1 in XGBoost indicates boundary ambiguity.

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Applying temperature scaling to XGBoost will increase macro_f1 by >1pp via improved confidence calibration.
supported_conjectures.basis: XGBoost confidence gap ~0.078 suggests room for calibration to better distinguish errors.
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Calibration experiments with post-hoc temperature scaling and resulting macro_f1

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Combining Delay_from_due_date and Monthly_Inhand_Salary into an interaction term will boost Standard recall by >5pp.
exploratory_leads.basis: Standard’s errors stem from its intermediate position in both features.
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Engineer interaction, retrain model, compare Standard recall

observations: Interest_Burden is the top SHAP feature with mean_abs_shap=0.3984, nearly twice the next feature (Interest_Rate=0.2007).
Grouped PFI ranks Delay_from_due_date second (importance_mean=0.0553) while SHAP ranks it fifth (mean_abs_shap=0.1537).
PDP for Credit_Mix shows predicted Good probability rising from 0.1648 at mix=0 to 0.3572 at mix=2 (Δ≈0.1924).
ALE for Interest_Rate shows Good ALE falling from +0.1119 at rate≈1.5 to –0.0444 by rate≈17.5, indicating a strong monotonic negative effect.
insights: The perfect overlap of top-5 features by both SHAP and grouped PFI (Interest_Burden, Delay_from_due_date, Interest_Rate, Credit_Mix, Outstanding_Debt) shows these signals drive both marginal and interaction-inclusive contributions, making them core to model decisions.

Payment_of_Min_Amount appears in the top-10 PFI (0.0262) but only 7th in SHAP (0.1171), implying its predictive value arises through interactions that SHAP’s marginal attributions diffuse across correlated features.
Monotonic decline in Good probability with rising Interest_Rate (via PDP/ALE) explains why higher rates continually push predictions away from Good toward Poor/Standard.

feature_importance_consensus.interpretation: Strong agreement on the top-5 features indicates the model relies centrally on interest and payment-delay metrics. Payment_of_Min_Amount’s isolation in PFI but not SHAP suggests it contributes via interactions rather than as an independent predictor.

Interest_Burden.class_direction: Higher Interest_Burden sharply increases Poor probability and decreases Standard, with a threshold effect around 120–280 before plateauing.
Interest_Burden.pdp_ale_divergence: PDP and ALE align closely, confirming minimal correlation bias.

Interest_Rate.class_direction: Increasing rates steadily decrease Good probability and raise Poor/Standard probabilities.

Credit_Mix.class_direction: Higher credit mix scores linearly raise Good probability and lower Standard/Poor probabilities.

Outstanding_Debt.class_direction: Higher debt increases Poor probability and reduces Good, with most change above ~500.
Outstanding_Debt.pdp_ale_divergence: Minor—ALE shows similar inflection points, boosting trust.

Delay_from_due_date.class_direction: Longer delays monotonically reduce Good probability and increase Poor/Standard probabilities.

cross_layer_validation.eda_to_xai: EDA highlighted Total_EMI_per_month and Monthly_Inhand_Salary as top MI features, but XAI shows the model learned Interest_Burden (#1 SHAP) instead of EMI, indicating feature transformations shifted importance away from raw EMI.
cross_layer_validation.training_to_xai: Standard class had the lowest recall and most confusion with Good/Poor; XAI shows Standard sits in mid-ranges of Interest_Burden and Interest_Rate where PDP curves cross, explaining its ambiguity and poor separability.
cross_layer_validation.fe_impact: The engineered EMI_to_Salary_Ratio appears only 12th in SHAP (0.0635) and 10th in PFI (0.0101), underperforming EDA expectations for top-5 impact, suggesting further tuning is needed.

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Increasing Credit_Mix from 0 (worst) to 2 (best) increases predicted Good probability by >0.19 on average
tested_predictions.basis: PDP for Credit_Mix: Good rises from 0.1648 at mix=0 to 0.3572 at mix=2
tested_predictions.how_to_test: Simulate data with fixed other features, vary Credit_Mix and measure predicted Good probability shift

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Payment_of_Min_Amount influences predictions primarily through interactions rather than marginal contributions
supported_conjectures.basis: grouped PFI importance=0.0262 vs mean_abs_shap=0.1171 rank7
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Analyze SHAP interaction values between Payment_of_Min_Amount and Delay_from_due_date

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: High Interest_Burden combined with high Interest_Rate synergistically amplifies probability of Poor classification
exploratory_leads.basis: Both are top-2 SHAP features but joint effect isn’t captured in one-dimensional PDP
exploratory_leads.implication: Model relies on compounded financial stress signals to flag Poor clients

Good.representative_profile: Row 2405’s high-confidence Good (0.9818) is driven by an above-average Num_Credit_Card (+1.0446), low Interest_Rate (+0.3890), and on-time Payment_of_Min_Amount (+0.2953).
Good.borderline_story: Row 5954 (Good, 0.342) sits on the Good/Standard razor’s edge: low Num_Credit_Card (shap −0.3935), modest Credit_Mix (+0.1533), and small Delay_from_due_date (+0.1486) produce a thin margin.
Good.worst_misclassification_story: Row 5549 (True=Good→Pred=Standard, conf=0.9488) is lured into Standard by high Interest_Rate (+0.3721), strong Credit_Mix (+0.3232), and elevated Interest_Burden (+0.3181).

Poor.representative_profile: Row 17984’s confident Poor (0.9537) is driven by extreme Interest_Burden (+0.7851), large Outstanding_Debt (+0.2874), and lengthy Delay_from_due_date (+0.2566).
Poor.borderline_story: Row 13116 (Poor, 0.3505) skirts the Poor/Standard border with moderate Interest_Burden (+0.4324), muted Changed_Credit_Limit (−0.2458), and mid-range Outstanding_Debt (+0.2362).
Poor.worst_misclassification_story: Row 18614 (True=Poor→Pred=Standard, conf=0.9482) is confidently mis‐routed by strong Credit_Mix (+0.4031), high Outstanding_Debt (+0.2877), and significant Interest_Burden (+0.2114).

Standard.representative_profile: Row 11747’s clear Standard (0.9638) reflects moderate Outstanding_Debt (+0.3745), mid-level Credit_Mix (+0.3278), and substantial Changed_Credit_Limit (+0.3140).
Standard.borderline_story: Row 17545 (Standard, 0.3503) lies in a fragile zone: low Credit_Mix (−0.7194), moderate Monthly_Inhand_Salary (+0.1344), and slight Delay_from_due_date (−0.0654) barely tip it to Standard.
Standard.worst_misclassification_story: Row 5978 (True=Standard→Pred=Good, conf=0.9758) is misled to Good by many Num_Credit_Card (+0.6675), low Interest_Rate (+0.3536), and elevated Interest_Burden (+0.2177).

confusion_patterns.dominant_direction: Good → Standard asymmetry: the model over-predicts Standard on Good cases under uncertainty (e.g. row 5549).

decision_boundary_analysis.thinnest_boundary: The Good vs Standard boundary is most fragile on Num_Credit_Card—small changes flip row 5954’s prediction.
decision_boundary_analysis.boundary_candidate_for_engineering: Bucketize Num_Credit_Card into low/mid/high bins (e.g. 0–2, 3–5, >5) to introduce clear split points around the observed threshold (~3 cards).

tested_predictions.hypothesis: For the borderline Good case at row 5954, increasing Num_Credit_Card to the representative level (as in row 2405) will increase predicted Good probability by >0.5
tested_predictions.basis: Row 5954 shap Num_Credit_Card=−0.3935 vs row 2405 shap=+1.0446
tested_predictions.how_to_test: Simulate row 5954 with higher Num_Credit_Card and measure probability shift

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Delay_from_due_date near 0–5 days creates a thin margin between Good and Standard
supported_conjectures.basis: Borderline rows 5954 (shap +0.1486) and 17545 (shap −0.0654) show small contributions
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Analyze confidence vs Delay_from_due_date across holdout at values 0–10 days

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Occupation category interacts with high Interest_Rate to systematically push Good cases into Standard
exploratory_leads.basis: Row 5549 included Occupation (shap +0.0933) alongside high Interest_Rate (+0.3721) in worst misclassification
exploratory_leads.implication: Model may underweight occupation effects—targeted encoding could reduce Good→Standard errors

feature_engineering_hypothesis.interactions_rationale: We engineered six domain-grounded interactions focusing on the highest-MI features per eda_insights (Total_EMI_per_month MI=0.5471; Monthly_Inhand_Salary MI=0.5187; Num_Credit_Inquiries MI=0.1114; Num_Credit_Card MI=0.1048; Delay-related features). EMI_to_Salary_Ratio fuses the top two MI features and, per exploratory_leads, is expected to exceed MI≈0.60. Savings_Rate captures residual cash after obligations. Inquiries_per_Credit_Card and Delay_Ratio normalize credit-seeking and payment discipline by exposure size. Interest_Burden and Balance_to_Salary_Ratio approximate interest cost and liquidity reserve. Ratios are stabilized (inf→NaN, median-imputed, clipped at p99/p1) to prevent outlier domination.
feature_engineering_hypothesis.dropped_features_rationale: Dropped Month (not in top_discriminative_features) and Credit_Utilization_Ratio (MI=0.0) as low-information noise. No constant or high-correlation features were present.
feature_engineering_hypothesis.expected_impact: These interactions should boost tree-model SHAP importance for EMI_to_Salary_Ratio into the top-5 and improve linear model separability by compressing distributions via log-transforms. Removing low-MI features reduces overfitting risk and speeds training.
eda_hypotheses_acted_on: exploratory_leads: log-transforming Num_Credit_Inquiries and Total_EMI_per_month implemented
tested_predictions: missingness indicator for Type_of_Loan not implemented because one-hot dummies implicitly capture missing rows
tested_predictions: XGBoost vs Logistic Regression performance hypothesis is model-level and outside FE scope

selection_justification: XGBoost shows the highest overall accuracy (0.68825) and slightly higher macro_f1 (0.682823) compared to Random Forest (0.682417). It handles the 'Standard' class slightly better with a macro_f1 score of 0.696, which is important as this is the largest class by support. The weighted_f1 is also the highest for XGBoost, suggesting that it maintains strong performance across all classes. Additionally, XGBoost's CV score (0.6652) and test score show a smaller gap compared to Random Forest, indicating lower risk of overfitting.
